items.myth: SVD and eigendecomposition of A are the same thing.
items.reality: SVD always exists and uses singular values; eigendecomposition applies to square A and uses eigenvalues. They coincide in special cases (e.g. symmetric positive semidefinite A), not in general.
items.example: A rectangular 100×5 data matrix has SVD but no eigenvalue decomposition of A itself.

items.myth: Small singular values are always noise and safe to discard.
items.reality: Small σᵢ can encode rare but important structure (outliers, long-tail topics, adversarial directions); blind truncation can remove signal.
items.example: In fraud detection, a low-variance direction may separate a tiny fraudulent cluster—dropping it harms recall.

items.myth: The matrices U and V from `np.linalg.svd` are unique.
items.reality: Singular values are unique (up to ordering), but singular vectors can flip sign and mix within degenerate subspaces (equal σᵢ).
items.example: If σ₁ = σ₂, any orthonormal basis of that 2D subspace is valid; comparing U across runs may show different column signs.
